items.q: Why did apartheid end in the early 1990s?
items.correct: A combination of internal resistance, international sanctions and economic pressure, and negotiations between de Klerk's government and Mandela's ANC produced a negotiated settlement and the 1994 election.
items.misconception: The United Nations voted to abolish apartheid and forced South Africa to comply.
items.random_wrong: Apartheid ended when Britain resumed direct rule over South Africa in 1990.
items.why: Students often assume an outside body simply ordered change, missing that the settlement was negotiated under combined internal and external pressure.

items.q: What was the policy of 'separate development' (the Bantustans)?
items.correct: Black South Africans were assigned to ethnic 'homelands' and stripped of South African citizenship, which the government used to justify excluding them from political rights.
items.misconception: The Bantustans were genuinely independent countries recognised by the international community.
items.random_wrong: The Bantustans were whites-only farming districts near Cape Town.
items.why: Students take the regime's language of 'independence' at face value; in reality no state other than South Africa recognised the homelands.

items.q: Why was Nelson Mandela imprisoned in 1964?
items.correct: He was convicted of sabotage at the Rivonia Trial for his role in Umkhonto we Sizwe's armed campaign against the apartheid state.
items.misconception: He was imprisoned simply for being a member of the ANC and making speeches.
items.random_wrong: He was imprisoned for organising the Soweto Uprising.
items.why: Students blur his sentence into general persecution; the Soweto Uprising happened in 1976, twelve years into his imprisonment.

items.q: In which year did the National Party win power and begin building apartheid?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1948. “1940” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What happened at Sharpeville on 21 March 1960?
items.correct: Police fired on an anti-pass-law crowd, killing 69 people
items.misconception: Nelson Mandela was released from prison
items.random_wrong: The first democratic election was held
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Police fired on an anti-pass-law crowd, killing 69 people. “Nelson Mandela was released from prison” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why was the Soweto Uprising of 1976 significant?
items.correct: A student protest against compulsory Afrikaans grew into nationwide resistance to apartheid
items.random_wrong: It was the first time the ANC was banned
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A student protest against compulsory Afrikaans grew into nationwide resistance to apartheid. “It ended apartheid immediately” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who became South Africa's first Black president in 1994?
items.why: Correct answer: Nelson Mandela. “Desmond Tutu” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Nelson Mandela released from prison?
items.why: Exact answer: 1990 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
